original_abstract:
Single-atom catalysts (SACs) offer a promising bridge between homogeneous and heterogeneous systems, yet the complex structural dynamics they undergo during operation remain poorly understood. This study investigates the evolution of active sites on a $Pd/TiO_2$-anatase SAC during the reverse water-gas shift (rWGS) reaction. By integrating kinetic analysis, in situ characterization, and theoretical modeling, we demonstrate that exposing the catalyst to $H_2$ at temperatures $\ge 350^\circ\text{C}$ leads to a significant increase in intrinsic rWGS activity. This activation is driven by the reduction of the $TiO_2$ support, which modifies the electronic structure of the Pd and induces a partial cleavage of the metal–support interface. These unique Pd sites effectively catalyze the reaction via the carboxyl pathway.

The high-temperature $H_2$ treatment also causes a fraction of the Pd single atoms to sinter into small, disordered, two-dimensional clusters ($\sim 1\text{ nm}$ diameter). These active configurations are reversible; oxidation at high temperatures re-disperses the clusters into single atoms and enhances activity by removing surface impurities. In contrast, reduction under CO results in severe sintering into large, crystalline nanoparticles ($\sim 5\text{ nm}$), which deactivates the catalyst. Our findings show that during the rWGS reaction, these competing evolutionary pathways co-exist, with $H_2$-driven activation ultimately determining the steady-state performance. These results highlight how the coordination environment and nuclearity of SACs are modulated by reaction conditions, providing critical insights for the rational design of stable and active atomically dispersed catalysts.
humanized_abstract:
Single-atom catalysts, defined as nanoparticles containing only a single active site per nanoparticle, bridge the homogeneous and heterogeneous catalysis realms. The structural changes they undergo during their activity are not fully understood. In this article, the activity sites on a Pd/TiO2-anatase single-atom catalyst toward the reverse water-gas shift (rWGS) reaction were investigated at different stages of the reaction. They conducted kinetic studies, in situ synchrotron X-ray absorption near edge structure experiments (XANES), and density functional theory (DFT) simulations. In situ XANES spectra showed that by exposing the catalyst to $H_2$ at $T\ge 350^\circ\text{C}$ led to a remarkable promotion of the intrinsic rWGS activity. Reduced TiO2 support enhanced the Pd electronic state as well as led to a partial dissociation of the atomic species at the metal–support interface, both being essential for enhancing the rWGS reaction activity of Pd sites through carboxyl route.

Reduction of highly coordinated atomically dispersed Pd on ferronitrile nanosheets for water gas shift A minority of the Pd single atoms are sintered into disordered 2D clusters ∼1 nm in diameter at high temperature in a stream of H 2 . The species and activity of the catalyst can be re-established by oxidation under similar conditions. Reductive under CO leads to massive sintering of Pd into larger crystalline 5 nm diameter nanoparticles which are inactive in rWGS. Analysis during rWGS shows that both evolutionary pathways occur in situ and that it is the hydrogen activation that governs the high performance of the catalyst. As such the reaction conditions strongly affect the coordination environment and nuclearity of SACs, a fundamental parameter influencing their stabilities and functions and it points out important guiding principles for the design of efficient and stable atomic species catalysts.

document.input:
Single-atom catalysts (SACs) offer a promising bridge between homogeneous and heterogeneous systems, yet the complex structural dynamics they undergo during operation remain poorly understood. These results highlight how the coordination environment and nuclearity of SACs are modulated by reaction conditions, providing critical insights for the rational design of stable and active atomically dispersed catalysts.
